ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pork sausage class (FOODON_03311737). No specific entry exists for Vietnamese blood-and-pork sausage (doi tiet). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers pork sausages as a category. No specific code for Vietnamese doi tiet (blood sausage variant) exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226845009 is the closest available concept for pork sausage. No specific concept for Vietnamese blood sausage with dipping sauce exists.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 2–3 sausage pieces with dipping sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Doi Tiet Cham Mam consists of two to three pieces of Vietnamese pork blood sausage (approximately 150 g total) accompanied by a small bowl of fermented fish sauce dip.

nutrient_source.title: Pork blood sausage, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 07068) and Vietnamese pork sausage composition estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for processed pork products
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese doi tiet (pork blood sausage with fish sauce dip) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork blood sausage (NDB 07068) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA processed pork product data. The dipping sauce (fermented fish sauce / mam) contributes additional sodium; sodium value is adjusted upward to reflect typical dish composition. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-and-starch sausage products — analogous estimate based on blood sausage / pork sausage GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese doi tiet. GI is estimated at 28 (low) based on the analogous category of processed pork sausage with rice/starch filler, referencing the University of Sydney GI Database entries for sausage-type products and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL. The low GI reflects the high protein and fat content which slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (28 × 7.2 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork processing and fermented fish sauce dip
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Doi Tiet Cham Mam due to its high sodium content (approximately 680 mg per 100 g), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure; the fermented fish sauce dip should be used sparingly or omitted.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low glycemic index; high saturated fat from pork
type2_diabetes.guidance: Doi Tiet Cham Mam has a low glycemic index (estimated 28) and moderate carbohydrate content, making glycemic impact manageable in controlled portions; however, the saturated fat and cholesterol content warrant attention for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who are also managing cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat, cholesterol, and high sodium from processed pork and fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Doi Tiet Cham Mam is high in saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g), cholesterol (95 mg/100 g), and sodium (680 mg/100 g), all of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or at elevated cardiovascular risk should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Doi Tiet Cham Mam (Dồi Tiết Chấm Mắm) is a traditional Vietnamese pork sausage dish made from a mixture of pork meat, pork blood, pork fat, cooked rice or rice flour, lemongrass, and aromatic herbs, stuffed into natural casings and then boiled or steamed until firm. The name translates roughly as 'blood sausage dipped in fermented sauce.' The sausage has a dense, slightly chewy texture with a rich, savoury flavour derived from the pork blood and spices, and a subtle herbal note from lemongrass. It is traditionally served sliced, accompanied by a dipping sauce made from fermented shrimp paste or fish sauce (mắm) mixed with chilli, lime, and sugar. In Singapore, this dish is served at Vietnamese restaurants such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant and is classified as a processed pork product. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-to-high energy food providing approximately 188 kcal per 100 g, with 14 g protein, 11.5 g fat, and 7.2 g carbohydrate. It is notably high in iron (6.8 mg/100 g) due to the pork blood content, and provides vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg/100 g). The sodium content is elevated (approximately 680 mg/100 g) due to both the sausage seasoning and the fermented dipping sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 28), reflecting the high protein and fat content. This dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets and contains fish allergens from the dipping sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese doi tiet (pork blood sausage with fish sauce dip) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for pork blood sausage (NDB 07068) with sodium adjusted to reflect the fermented fish sauce dipping component. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for processed pork sausage products. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 07068, pork blood sausage) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA processed pork product data. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this dish. 2. Sodium value (680 mg/100 g) is adjusted upward from the base sausage value to account for the fermented fish sauce (mắm) dipping component, which is a significant sodium contributor. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this product category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for pork blood sausage. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 4. GI is estimated at 28 (low) based on analogous processed pork sausage entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for doi tiet. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Meat and alternatives', which accurately reflects the primary composition of this dish as a processed pork sausage product. 6. This dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. The dipping sauce contains fish allergens. 7. The cuisine is correctly classified as Vietnamese, served in a Singapore restaurant context.
